script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Welcome to DataBard! Today we're looking at the ecommerce schema — 4 tables, health score 58 out of 100. Let's dig in.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: Mixed bag here. 71% test coverage, 50% documented. Some gaps to address.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: Let's start with the fires. There are 2 tables I'm worried about.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: orders is critical — 1 failing test and 2 tables depend on it. If this breaks, it cascades.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: For context, orders is the core transaction table — every purchase flows through here.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: order_items has 1 failing test — positive_quantity. Needs attention.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: products has zero quality tests. That's 25% of the schema flying blind.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Only 50% of tables are documented. products and order_items have no description at all.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: On the bright side — customers is well-documented with all tests passing. That's the standard to aim for.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Data flow: 3 lineage connections. orders is the busiest node with 3 connections.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: All lineage is internal to this schema. That's good for isolation, but make sure upstream sources are monitored.

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: That's the ecommerce schema — health score 58, 4 tables, 7 tests. Thanks for listening.

script.speaker: Morgan
script.text: Priority one: fix those 2 failing tests. Priority two: get test coverage above 71%. Until next time.
